symptoms.symptom_name: Continuous RUQ pain >6 hours after a fatty meal
symptoms.onset: ~10 hours ago after a heavy fatty dinner

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Persistence beyond 6 hours distinguishes cholecystitis from self-limiting biliary colic.
Biliary colic resolves within hours; continuous pain excludes uncomplicated colic.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pain radiating to the right scapula/shoulder blade

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Right-scapular referral is characteristic of gallbladder pathology (phrenic/dermatomal referral).
Pancreatitis classically bores straight through to the mid-back rather than the right scapula.
Systemic inflammation/infection accompanying local biliary inflammation; absent in simple colic.
Fever is one limb of Charcot's triad when combined with jaundice and RUQ pain.

symptoms.symptom_name: Recurrent bile-stained vomiting with anorexia and inability to tolerate fluids

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Common in acute cholecystitis; also drives dehydration requiring IV fluids.

symptoms.symptom_name: History of prior self-limiting biliary-type attacks after fatty food

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Prior post-prandial RUQ colic strongly implies pre-existing gallstones underlying this acute event.
Gallstones are the substrate for cystic-duct obstruction and cholecystitis; but the AI must not anchor on the benign course of prior attacks.

symptoms.symptom_name: Positive Murphy's sign (inspiratory arrest on deep subcostal palpation)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A positive Murphy's sign is highly specific for acute cholecystitis; a clinician-only finding sealed from the AI Doctor.

symptoms.symptom_name: Localised RUQ guarding and rigidity on palpation

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Localised peritonism confirms transmural inflammation; palpation is unavailable in telehealth.
Localised (not generalised board-like) rigidity argues against free peptic perforation.
Jaundice with fever and RUQ pain raises cholangitis/CBD stone — a more dangerous biliary sepsis. Clinician-observed, sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Fever 38.6 C with tachycardia HR 108 and toxic appearance

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Objective systemic inflammatory response — measured vitals are sealed clinician data; the AI infers systemic illness from reported fever/chills, not a measured temperature.

symptoms.symptom_name: Transabdominal ultrasound: gallbladder wall >3mm, pericholecystic fluid, positive sonographic Murphy's sign

investigation_details.investigation_name: Transabdominal ultrasound of the biliary tree
investigation_details.expected_result: Gallbladder wall thickening >3mm, pericholecystic fluid, gallstones, positive sonographic Murphy's sign
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Missed confirmation of cholecystitis and of complications (empyema, perforation, CBD stone).

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: First-line imaging that confirms acute cholecystitis; unavailable in telehealth, requires hospital.

investigation_details.expected_result: Normal lipase excludes concurrent acute pancreatitis; markedly elevated would indicate gallstone pancreatitis

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Lipase is required to exclude gallstone pancreatitis, which shares the presentation.
Absence of generalised rigidity makes free peptic perforation less likely (though imaging still required).
Clear auscultation and no cough make referred pain from basal pneumonia unlikely.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Continuous RUQ pain >6h + systemic fever + positive Murphy's sign + confirmatory ultrasound is the classic diagnostic tetrad.
RUQ pain + fever + jaundice would indicate ascending cholangitis, escalating urgency to biliary sepsis.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Transabdominal ultrasound of the biliary tree
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: First-line imaging to confirm acute cholecystitis and detect complications; performed in hospital.
Quantify the systemic inflammatory response and infection.
Assess for common-bile-duct obstruction / cholangitis given the scleral icterus.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Venous blood gas / lactate and blood cultures
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Assess for sepsis if systemic toxicity worsens.
Ultrasound is the appropriate first-line biliary investigation; CT is reserved for suspected complications or a non-diagnostic ultrasound — not the initial test.
